parts.purpose: Hook — acids and bases are everywhere, defined by H⁺ transfer

blocks.body: Squeeze a lemon into water — the sour taste comes from citric acid releasing H⁺ ions. Rub baking soda between your fingers — the slippery feel comes from a base absorbing H⁺ ions. Acids and bases are defined by one tiny particle: the hydrogen ion (H⁺).

Acids and bases are everywhere: your stomach acid (pH 1.5), your blood (pH 7.4), your soap (pH 10), your drain cleaner (pH 14). The pH scale measures H⁺ concentration, and even tiny shifts can mean the difference between life and death for your cells.

This lesson covers what acids and bases ARE (H⁺ donors and acceptors), how we measure them (the pH scale), and what happens when they meet (neutralization). It's some of the most practical chemistry you'll learn.

blocks.title: Taste and Touch
blocks.body: Acids taste sour — lemon, vinegar, orange juice. Bases taste bitter and feel slippery — soap, baking soda dissolved in water. (Safety note: never taste unknown chemicals! These examples are everyday foods and household items.)

Put red cabbage juice in three glasses. Add vinegar to one (turns pink — acid). Add baking soda solution to another (turns blue-green — base). The third is neutral (stays purple). You just made a pH indicator from a vegetable.

Indicators change color based on H⁺ concentration. Red/pink = acidic (lots of H⁺). Blue/green = basic (few H⁺). This color change is a chemical reaction triggered by the presence or absence of hydrogen ions.
Acids taste sour. Bases taste bitter. Your stomach is a bag of acid strong enough to dissolve metal. Your blood is slightly basic. You are a walking chemistry experiment in acid-base balance.

Your stomach acid (HCl) has a pH around 1.5 — acidic enough to dissolve a nail. Your stomach lining replaces itself every few days because the acid literally eats through it. Your body is actively losing a fight against its own digestive system and winning by regenerating faster.
The entire acid-base story boils down to one particle: H⁺. Who's giving it? That's the acid. Who's taking it? That's the base. One ion to rule them all.

parts.part_title: What Makes an Acid and a Base
parts.purpose: Core — Arrhenius and Brønsted-Lowry definitions, strong vs weak

blocks.title: Arrhenius Definition (Simple)
blocks.body: Acid: produces H⁺ ions in water. HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. Base: produces OH⁻ ions in water. NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻.

blocks.title: Brønsted-Lowry Definition (Broader)
blocks.body: Acid: donates H⁺ (proton donor). Base: accepts H⁺ (proton acceptor). This definition works even without water — it's more universal.

items.label: Strong Acids (fully dissociate)
items.body: Every molecule releases H⁺. HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄. 100% ionization. Strong doesn't mean concentrated — it means complete dissociation.

items.label: Weak Acids (partially dissociate)
items.body: Only some molecules release H⁺. CH₃COOH (acetic acid/vinegar), H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid). Equilibrium between ionized and un-ionized forms.

items.label: Strong Bases (fully dissociate)
items.body: Every unit releases OH⁻. NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂. Complete ionization.

items.label: Weak Bases (partially dissociate)
items.body: Only partially accept H⁺. NH₃ (ammonia). Equilibrium.

blocks.body: Strong ≠ concentrated. Strong means 100% dissociation. You can have a dilute strong acid (a tiny amount of HCl in lots of water — fully dissociated but not many H⁺ overall) or a concentrated weak acid (lots of vinegar — most molecules stay intact).

items.label: Lemon juice (citric acid)
items.hints: Weak acid. Only partially ionized. pH ~2 (acidic but not fully dissociated).

items.label: Battery acid (H₂SO₄)
items.hints: Strong acid. Fully dissociates. pH can be below 0 when concentrated.

items.label: Ammonia cleaner (NH₃)
items.hints: Weak base. Partially accepts H⁺ from water.

items.label: Drain cleaner (NaOH)
items.hints: Strong base. Fully dissociates to Na⁺ + OH⁻.

blocks.body: Common strong acids to memorize: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HClO₄. Common strong bases: NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂, Ba(OH)₂. Everything else is typically weak.

blocks.title: Acids Give, Bases Take
blocks.body: An acid walks into a reaction with an H⁺ in its hand and says 'does anyone want this?' A base walks in and says 'I'll take it.' That's the Brønsted-Lowry definition. Proton donor meets proton acceptor. Chemistry's most straightforward transaction.

Strong acids are aggressive givers — they shove ALL their H⁺ into solution. HCl doesn't hold back. 100% dissociation. Weak acids are reluctant — vinegar says 'hmm, maybe I'll release SOME H⁺ but keep most for myself.' About 1% ionization. It's the difference between someone who donates everything to charity and someone who drops a coin in the jar.

H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) is the most produced chemical in the world — over 250 million tonnes per year. It's used in fertilizers, batteries, metal processing, and manufacturing. If industrial civilization has a lifeblood, it's sulfuric acid. Not exactly a feel-good fact, but an impressive one.

questions.question: According to Brønsted-Lowry, what defines an acid?

options.B: A proton (H⁺) donor — it gives away hydrogen ions

questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry: acid = H⁺ donor, base = H⁺ acceptor. This is broader than Arrhenius (which requires water). Not all H-containing substances are acids (e.g., CH₄ doesn't donate H⁺).

questions.question: HCl is a strong acid and CH₃COOH (vinegar) is a weak acid. What's the difference?

options.B: HCl dissociates 100% in water while CH₃COOH dissociates only ~1%
options.D: Weak acids don't produce H⁺ at all

questions.explanation: Strong = 100% dissociation (every molecule releases H⁺). Weak = partial dissociation (most molecules stay intact, equilibrium). Concentration is separate from strength. Even weak acids produce H⁺ — just less per molecule.

parts.purpose: Teach pH as -log[H⁺], the 0-14 scale, and what the numbers mean

blocks.title: pH
blocks.body: pH = -log[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the concentration of hydrogen ions in mol/L. In practice: pH measures how acidic or basic a solution is on a scale from 0 to 14.

items.label: pH < 7 = Acidic
items.body: More H⁺ than pure water. Lower pH = more acidic. pH 1 (battery acid) is extremely acidic. pH 6 (milk) is mildly acidic.

items.label: pH = 7 = Neutral
items.body: Pure water. Equal H⁺ and OH⁻. Neither acidic nor basic.

items.label: pH > 7 = Basic (Alkaline)
items.body: Fewer H⁺ than pure water (more OH⁻). Higher pH = more basic. pH 8 (seawater) is mildly basic. pH 14 (NaOH) is extremely basic.

blocks.title: The Logarithmic Trap
blocks.body: Each pH unit represents a 10× change in H⁺ concentration. pH 3 has 10× more H⁺ than pH 4, and 100× more than pH 5. A seemingly small change from pH 7 to pH 5 is actually a 100-fold increase in acidity. The scale is deceptive — small numbers hide enormous differences.

blocks.prompt: Create a pH scale poster using real-world examples at each pH level (0-14). Photograph actual household items and arrange them along the scale. Color-code acid vs base regions. Best poster becomes the official pH reference!

blocks.title: Test pH at Home
blocks.body: Red cabbage juice is a natural pH indicator. Blend red cabbage in water, strain, and add small amounts of household substances to see the color change.

Your blood pH must stay between 7.35 and 7.45. A shift to 7.0 (seemingly tiny: 0.35 units) means a 2.2× increase in H⁺ — potentially fatal. Your body has elaborate buffering systems to prevent this. The logarithmic scale means small pH changes represent large chemical shifts.

blocks.title: The Deceptive Scale
blocks.body: pH looks friendly: 0 to 14, nice round numbers. But it's LOGARITHMIC. Each step is 10× the actual change. pH 2 to pH 4 isn't 'twice as acidic' — it's 100× LESS acidic. The scale lies to your face with every number.

Your stomach acid (pH ~1.5) is about 3,000,000× more acidic than your blood (pH ~7.4). These two fluids exist inches apart in your body, separated by a mucus lining that replaces itself constantly. Your body is basically running a chemical containment facility 24/7.

Fun party trick: the pH of Coca-Cola (~2.5) is close to vinegar (~3). Your teeth enamel starts dissolving at pH 5.5. So yes, soda is literally dissolving your teeth. Slowly. This is not fun. But it is chemistry.

questions.question: A solution has pH 3 and another has pH 5. How much more acidic is pH 3?

options.B: 100× more acidic — each pH unit is 10×, so 2 units = 10² = 100×

questions.explanation: pH is logarithmic: each unit = 10× change in [H⁺]. Two units apart: 10² = 100× difference. pH 3 has 100 times more H⁺ than pH 5.

questions.question: Blood pH must stay near 7.4. Is blood acidic, basic, or neutral?

options.C: Slightly basic — pH > 7 means more OH⁻ than H⁺
options.D: It varies too much to classify

questions.explanation: pH 7.0 = neutral. Anything above 7.0 = basic. Blood at 7.4 is slightly basic (alkaline). Even this tiny deviation from 7.0 matters — your enzymes are optimized for this exact pH.

parts.purpose: Teach neutralization reactions, salt + water products, real-world applications

blocks.title: Neutralization
blocks.body: When an acid and a base react, H⁺ from the acid combines with OH⁻ from the base to form water (H₂O). The remaining ions form a salt. General: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

blocks.title: The Pattern
blocks.body: The H⁺ and OH⁻ always combine to make H₂O. The leftover cation (from the base) and anion (from the acid) form the salt. Different acid-base pairs produce different salts.

items.definition: NaCl + H₂O + CO₂ (baking soda neutralizing acid — with fizz!)

blocks.body: Neutralization is a double replacement reaction where H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O is the driving force. The 'salt' isn't necessarily table salt — it's any ionic compound formed from the leftover ions. The reaction moves pH toward 7 (neutral).

blocks.title: Real-World Neutralization
blocks.body: Antacids (CaCO₃, Mg(OH)₂) neutralize excess stomach acid. Lime (Ca(OH)₂) neutralizes acidic soil for farming. Baking soda neutralizes acid spills in labs. Your blood's buffering system is continuous neutralization maintaining pH 7.4.

Acids and bases transfer H⁺ ions. But there's another kind of transfer that's equally important: ELECTRON transfer. When electrons move between atoms in a reaction, that's oxidation-reduction (redox). It powers batteries, causes rust, and drives photosynthesis. That's next.

blocks.title: Neutralize at Home
blocks.body: Add baking soda (base) to vinegar (acid). The fizzing is CO₂ produced when the acid neutralizes the bicarbonate. The resulting solution is closer to pH 7 than either starting material. You just performed a neutralization reaction.

Antacid tablets work the same way. Dissolve one in water with a few drops of lemon juice. The bubbling/fizzing is the base neutralizing the acid. The resulting solution is less acidic — exactly what your stomach needs when you have heartburn.

Every neutralization produces salt + water. The specific salt depends on which acid and base you use. This predictability is what makes neutralization useful: you can choose exactly which salt to produce by choosing your reactants.
Next: oxidation-reduction reactions — where ELECTRONS transfer instead of protons. Rust, batteries, respiration, and photosynthesis are all redox.

blocks.title: Acid + Base = Drama Then Peace
blocks.body: Acid walks in with H⁺. Base walks in with OH⁻. They meet, react violently (sometimes literally with heat and bubbling), and produce... water. The most boring substance in chemistry. Plus whatever salt falls out. It's like a dramatic soap opera that ends with everyone sitting quietly at a table eating NaCl.

items.label: Antacids
items.body: Your stomach: pH ~1.5. The Tums you chewed: CaCO₃ (base). Result: neutralization → relief. You just did chemistry with your esophagus.

items.label: Bee Stings vs Wasp Stings
items.body: Bee venom is acidic → treat with baking soda (base). Wasp venom is basic → treat with vinegar (acid). Neutralization as first aid. Know your attacker's pH.

blocks.body: The Dead Sea has pH ~9 (basic) and is 34% salt — roughly 10× saltier than the ocean. It's what happens when millennia of mineral-laden water flows in and evaporates, concentrating salts. You float in it because the dense salt water is denser than your body. Chemistry makes you buoyant.
Acid-base: proton transfer. Next up: ELECTRON transfer — oxidation and reduction. Where things rust, batteries charge, and energy flows.

questions.question: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. What type of reaction is this?
questions.explanation: Acid (HCl) + base (NaOH) → salt (NaCl) + water. The H⁺ from HCl combines with OH⁻ from NaOH to form H₂O. The Na⁺ and Cl⁻ form the salt. Classic neutralization (a specific type of double replacement).

questions.question: If you react H₂SO₄ with KOH, predict the products.

options.B: K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O — K⁺ pairs with SO₄²⁻, H⁺ pairs with OH⁻

questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O. The H⁺ ions combine with OH⁻ to form H₂O. The K⁺ and SO₄²⁻ form the salt K₂SO₄. Two KOH needed because H₂SO₄ has 2 H⁺ to neutralize.
